baseline_or_market_size: Approx. 52.8 mb/d world oil-consumption equivalent in 1973 from Energy Institute/OWID oil consumption converted at 620 TWh per 1 mb/d-year; total-liquids denominator would be somewhat higher.
spare_capacity_buffer: Low practical buffer for targeted importers; U.S. spare capacity had diminished and OAPEC spare barrels were politically withheld.
inventory_or_policy_buffer: No IEA collective stockdraw system yet; response relied on allocation controls, speed limits, conservation, and later creation of emergency-stock institutions.
route_substitution_constraint: Embargo/allocation shock rather than a physical chokepoint closure; substitution depended on non-OAPEC supply and reallocation, not route bypass.
caveats: Price change is World Bank monthly crude-oil average deflated with FRED CPI, peak during event window vs prior-3-month average; pre-1980 benchmark is less comparable than Brent/WTI era. Denominator is oil-consumption equivalent, not a fully harmonized total-liquids balance.

baseline_or_market_size: Approx. 60.0 mb/d world oil-consumption equivalent in 1979 from Energy Institute/OWID conversion; acute Iranian output loss occurred before full 1979-80 price shock peak.
spare_capacity_buffer: Partial offset from other producers, especially Saudi/OPEC, but tight demand, precautionary stockbuilding, and quality/location constraints limited effective buffer.
inventory_or_policy_buffer: IEA existed but no comparable collective emergency release; private and government stockbuilding amplified the market squeeze rather than buffering it cleanly.
route_substitution_constraint: Producer-regime-collapse outage; no route closure, but Iranian crude quality and contract replacement constraints mattered.
caveats: Price column covers Nov. 1978-Apr. 1979 acute window; Fed History notes prices more than doubled from Apr. 1979 to Apr. 1980, so this row understates the full second-oil-shock price episode.

baseline_or_market_size: Approx. 57.4 mb/d world oil-consumption equivalent in 1980 from Energy Institute/OWID conversion; global demand was already weakening after the 1979 shock.
spare_capacity_buffer: Some OPEC replacement supply was available, but the outage hit two Gulf producers soon after the Iranian Revolution and usable spare capacity was politically and logistically constrained.
inventory_or_policy_buffer: IEA emergency system existed but no clear collective stock release for this episode in the source set; high prices and recession/demand destruction became a major adjustment channel.
route_substitution_constraint: Regional war damaged export infrastructure and raised Gulf shipping risk; Strait did not fully close, so substitution was supply/terminal replacement more than route bypass.
caveats: Measured price rise is small because oil prices were already extremely elevated before the war outbreak. Treat as incremental shock on top of the 1978-80 regime shift.

baseline_or_market_size: Approx. 61.1 mb/d world oil-consumption equivalent in 1990 from Energy Institute/OWID conversion; EIA says nearly all Kuwaiti and Iraqi output went offline immediately.
spare_capacity_buffer: Meaningful offset from Saudi/OPEC spare capacity and later restoration of market confidence; buffer was stronger than in the 1970s.
inventory_or_policy_buffer: IEA first collective action/contingency plan in Jan. 1991; emergency measures and stockdraws helped offset the loss, with institutional sources citing roughly 2.5 mb/d equivalent planned response.
route_substitution_constraint: Country-export outage and war-risk shock, not a Hormuz closure; replacement barrels from other OPEC members were the key substitution path.
caveats: End date uses start of Operation Desert Storm as acute pre-response window; Kuwaiti infrastructure recovery continued long after the price spike.

baseline_or_market_size: Approx. 70.5 mb/d world oil-consumption equivalent in 2002 from Energy Institute/OWID conversion; Venezuelan production fell from roughly 3.4 mb/d before the strike to below 1 mb/d at the trough in institutional accounts.
spare_capacity_buffer: Moderate global buffer, but heavy/sour crude quality and U.S. Gulf Coast refinery fit reduced substitutability; shock overlapped with Iraq-war and Nigeria risks.
inventory_or_policy_buffer: No IEA collective release identified for this strike; adjustment came through replacement crude, inventories, and demand/price response.
route_substitution_constraint: Not a route shock; substitution constrained by crude quality and refinery configuration rather than maritime chokepoint capacity.
caveats: Attribution is messy because the strike coincided with Iraq-war risk and other disruptions; price change should not be read as Venezuela-only impact.

commodity_scope: crude oil, refined products, and refining/logistics
baseline_or_market_size: Approx. 75.8 mb/d world oil-consumption equivalent in 2005 from Energy Institute/OWID conversion; U.S. Gulf offshore output around 1.5 mb/d was shut in at peak and refining/product logistics were also disrupted.
unit: mb/d gross peak crude supply loss; refining outages larger but not included in share
spare_capacity_buffer: Crude supply buffer was less binding than refining/product logistics; product imports, refinery recovery, and fuel waivers mattered more than upstream spare capacity alone.
inventory_or_policy_buffer: IEA coordinated 60 million barrel release at 2 mb/d for 30 days; U.S. DOE SPR sale awarded about 11 million barrels after Katrina.
route_substitution_constraint: Domestic infrastructure/refining shock; route substitution through imports was possible but constrained by product specs, port/refinery outages, and delivery timing.
caveats: A crude-only disrupted share understates the shock because refinery capacity, pipelines, ports, and refined-product supply were central.

baseline_or_market_size: Approx. 78.4 mb/d world oil-consumption equivalent in 2011 from Energy Institute/OWID conversion; IEA estimated 132 million barrels of light sweet crude removed by end-May and expected disruption to persist.
spare_capacity_buffer: Headline spare capacity existed, especially Saudi, but quality mismatch mattered because Libyan light sweet crude fit specific European refineries.
inventory_or_policy_buffer: IEA made 60 million barrels available over one month to offset Libyan disruption while waiting for other supply to reach market.
route_substitution_constraint: Not a route closure; substitution constrained by crude grade, refinery configuration, and Mediterranean sourcing patterns.
caveats: Small global share had outsized market relevance because crude quality and refinery fit mattered; duration field uses broad civil-war disruption window, not only Feb-May cumulative loss window.

commodity_scope: crude oil processing and production capacity
baseline_or_market_size: Global petroleum-liquids market around 100 mb/d in 2019; EIA/CRS report a temporary 5.7 mb/d Saudi outage, roughly 5% of global supply.
spare_capacity_buffer: Saudi inventories, rapid repairs, and available capacity elsewhere buffered the market; however spare capacity was partly inside the affected Saudi system.
inventory_or_policy_buffer: No IEA collective release; Saudi commercial/strategic storage and fast restoration were the main buffer.
route_substitution_constraint: Infrastructure-processing shock rather than route shock; route substitution was not binding, but facility concentration created single-point risk.
caveats: Monthly average price metric masks the intraday/daily spike and fast reversal after repair assurances; use daily Brent for market-reaction charts.

case_name: 2022 Russia invasion, sanctions, and buyer strike
baseline_or_market_size: Global petroleum-liquids market near 99-100 mb/d in 2022; IEA March 2022 warned 3 mb/d of Russian oil output could be shut in from April, while pre-shock Russian exports were about 5 mb/d crude and 2.85 mb/d products.
unit: mb/d potential shut-in / at-risk output estimate
spare_capacity_buffer: Spare capacity was concentrated in a few OPEC+ producers and constrained by politics, sanctions, quality, refinery fit, shipping, and insurance; U.S. shale response was slower than acute window.
inventory_or_policy_buffer: IEA 2022 collective actions ultimately totaled 182.7 million barrels; U.S. SPR releases were also material, but stockdraw timing differed from physical Russian rerouting.
route_substitution_constraint: High substitution friction: sanctions, self-sanctioning, insurance, tanker availability, longer voyages, and destination switching turned this into a rerouting/regime-change shock rather than a clean physical outage.
caveats: Use 3 mb/d as IEA risk estimate, not confirmed sustained physical loss. Russia redirected large volumes, so exposed flow, realized shut-in, and rerouted flow must remain separate in later ranking.
